data.title: Core Scientific Pivots Pecos Campus Toward AI Data Centers And Debt-Funded Growth
data.description:
• Core Scientific (NasdaqGS:CORZ) is expanding its Pecos, Texas campus into a large-scale AI data center, repurposing capacity previously focused on bitcoin mining.
  • The company plans to use significant existing power infrastructure and behind-the-meter solutions to support high-demand AI workloads.
  • Institutional investors, including Oaktree Capital Management, have increased their stakes in Core Scientific alongside this corporate transformation.

For investors, this shift positions Core Scientific away from a pure bitcoin mining profile toward broader AI infrastructure services. The Pecos campus move ties the company to demand for data center capacity that supports training and running AI models, an area many large enterprises and cloud providers are prioritizing. The reuse of established power capacity may matter for both economics and speed to market compared with greenfield builds.

Alongside this, growing interest from institutional holders such as Oaktree Capital Management puts more attention on execution, governance, and capital allocation at NasdaqGS:CORZ. Readers may want to track how quickly AI-related capacity at Pecos ramps, how contracts are structured, and how the mix between mining and AI workloads evolves over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Core Scientific by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Core Scientific.

We've flagged 2 risks for Core Scientific. See which could impact your investment.

This Pecos expansion signals that Core Scientific wants most of its power portfolio pointed at high performance computing rather than bitcoin mining, which shifts the business model closer to AI-focused data center operators like Equinix, Digital Realty, or NVIDIA-linked infrastructure partners. The US$3.3b senior secured notes due 2031 highlight how capital intensive this pivot is, with secured, guaranteed, callable debt sitting high in the capital structure. For equity holders, that can increase sensitivity to execution on build-out timelines, customer commitments, and power availability, because debt holders are paid first.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Core Scientific Narrative

  • The Pecos build-out and power expansion directly tie to the narrative that reallocating capacity from bitcoin mining into HPC infrastructure can support AI hosting contracts such as the multi year CoreWeave deal.
  • Relying heavily on a single anchor customer and large secured borrowings could challenge the narrative if AI-focused workloads or contract utilization fall short of expectations.
  • The scale of the Pecos campus and the new fixed income funding may not be fully captured in earlier narrative assessments of power capacity and balance sheet structure.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Core Scientific to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Large senior secured debt of US$3.3b increases fixed obligations, so any delays or shortfalls in AI data center utilization could put pressure on cash flow.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks, including balance sheet concerns and limited cash runway, which may be more important as Core Scientific ramps capital-heavy AI projects.
  • 🎁 The Pecos campus repurposes existing power infrastructure, which could offer cost and timing advantages compared with building entirely new AI data centers from scratch.
  • 🎁 Growing interest from institutional investors such as Oaktree Capital Management suggests that professional investors see the AI infrastructure pivot as a thesis worth monitoring closely.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, pay attention to how quickly Core Scientific turns the Pecos power capacity into contracted, revenue producing AI workloads, how concentrated those contracts are, and whether terms lock in attractive economics over time. The pace at which the US$3.3b in notes is deployed into productive assets, and any updates on liquidity or refinancing options, will help clarify whether the new capital structure supports or strains the equity story. It is also worth watching how Core Scientific positions itself competitively compared with other AI-focused data center operators on pricing, power efficiency, and reliability.

data.title: Should Income Investors Look At d'Amico International Shipping S.A. (BIT:DIS) Before Its Ex-Dividend?
data.description:
d'Amico International Shipping S.A. (BIT:DIS) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 4 days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Accordingly, d'Amico International Shipping investors that purchase the stock on or after the 4th of May will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 6th of May.

The company's next dividend payment will be US$0.2295 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of US$0.43 to shareholders. Calculating the last year's worth of payments shows that d'Amico International Shipping has a trailing yield of 4.5% on the current share price of €7.715. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! As a result, readers should always check whether d'Amico International Shipping has been able to grow its dividends, or if the dividend might be cut.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. d'Amico International Shipping is paying out an acceptable 54% of its profit, a common payout level among most companies. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether d'Amico International Shipping generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 56% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

View our latest analysis for d'Amico International Shipping

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. That's why it's comforting to see d'Amico International Shipping's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 41% per annum for the past five years. The current payout ratio suggests a good balance between rewarding shareholders with dividends, and reinvesting in growth. Earnings per share have been growing quickly and in combination with some reinvestment and a middling payout ratio, the stock may have decent dividend prospects going forwards.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. d'Amico International Shipping has delivered an average of 3.2% per year annual increase in its dividend, based on the past 10 years of dividend payments. It's good to see both earnings and the dividend have improved - although the former has been rising much quicker than the latter, possibly due to the company reinvesting more of its profits in growth.

To Sum It Up

Has d'Amico International Shipping got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? It's good to see earnings are growing, since all of the best dividend stocks grow their earnings meaningfully over the long run. However, we'd also note that d'Amico International Shipping is paying out more than half of its earnings and cash flow as profits, which could limit the dividend growth if earnings growth slows. Overall, it's not a bad combination, but we feel that there are likely more attractive dividend prospects out there.

So while d'Amico International Shipping looks good from a dividend perspective, it's always worthwhile being up to date with the risks involved in this stock. For instance, we've identified 4 warning signs for d'Amico International Shipping (1 can't be ignored) you should be aware of.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess ARCHION (TSE:543A) After Its Sharp Share Price Slide?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether ARCHION at ¥326 is starting to look like value or still too risky for your portfolio, this article walks through what the current share price may imply.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with returns of an 11.7% decline over 7 days, a 15.8% decline over 30 days and an 18.1% decline year to date, adding to a 26.7% decline over 1 year, a 39.6% decline over 3 years and a 64.0% decline over 5 years.
  • These price moves sit against a backdrop of continuing interest in capital goods companies on the Tokyo Stock Exchange, as investors reassess where value and risk sit in the sector. ARCHION is also part of an industrial group where sentiment can shift quickly when broader economic expectations or funding conditions change, which helps frame the recent share price performance.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation framework, ARCHION scores a 5 out of 6 for being undervalued. Next you will see how traditional methods like DCF and multiples compare, before finishing with a more rounded way to think about what that score really means.

Find out why ARCHION's -26.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: ARCHION Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value. It is essentially asking what ARCHION's future ¥ cash flows are worth in today's terms.

For ARCHION, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of ¥73.5 million, so the valuation leans heavily on future projections. Analyst data and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to free cash flow of ¥87.0 million in 2026 and ¥369.0 million in 2028, with projections continuing out to 2035, all kept in ¥ and adjusted back to today using a discount rate.

Adding these discounted cash flows together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of ¥1,266.19 per share. Compared with the current share price of ¥326, this implies ARCHION trades at a 74.3% discount to that DCF estimate. On this metric alone, the stock appears significantly undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests ARCHION is undervalued by 74.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 14 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for ARCHION.

Approach 2: ARCHION Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shortcut because it links what you pay per share directly to the earnings the business generates. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on how they see the trade off between growth potential, earnings quality and risk.

ARCHION currently trades on a P/E of 11.5x. That sits below the Machinery industry average P/E of about 14.9x and also below the peer group average of 15.8x. On simple comparisons with the sector and peers, the share price is not demanding relative to current earnings.

Simply Wall St's Fair Ratio for ARCHION is 22.8x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a more appropriate P/E might be, after accounting for factors such as the company's earnings profile, its industry, profit margins, market capitalization and key risks. Because it is tailored to the company, the Fair Ratio can be more informative than a broad industry or peer average, which treat all Machinery names as if they face the same prospects and risks.

Comparing ARCHION's actual P/E of 11.5x with the Fair Ratio of 22.8x points to the shares trading below that Fair Ratio estimate.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Hilton (HLT) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Growth Narrative After Q1 2026 Results
data.description:
Hilton Worldwide Holdings (HLT) opened 2026 with Q1 revenue of US$2.9b and basic EPS of US$1.68, setting the tone for how its fee driven model is translating into bottom line results. Over the last five reported quarters, revenue has ranged from US$1.1b to US$2.9b while basic EPS moved between US$1.25 and US$2.08, giving investors a clear view of how earnings have tracked alongside a growing top line. With trailing net profit margins reported at 29.7%, down from 33% a year earlier, this latest print lands in a context where revenue scale is expanding, but profit conversion is under closer scrutiny.

See our full analysis for Hilton Worldwide Holdings.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up against the prevailing growth and risk narratives investors follow around Hilton.

See what the community is saying about Hilton Worldwide Holdings

Revenue near US$5.2b over last 12 months

  • Analysts' bullish narrative leans on aggressive global expansion and an asset light model, and the current scale helps that case but the slip in margin adds some friction, as:
    • Revenue forecasts of roughly 21.2% a year and earnings forecasts of about 13.9% a year build on this US$5.2b revenue base.

Bulls argue the real story is how Hilton’s global pipeline and fee heavy model translate this US$5.2b revenue base into future earnings strength, so it can be worth seeing how that full case is laid out in 🐂 Hilton Worldwide Holdings Bull Case

data.title: We Believe Pinewood Technologies Group's (LON:PINE) Earnings Are A Poor Guide For Its Profitability
data.description:
We didn't see Pinewood Technologies Group PLC's (LON:PINE) stock surge when it reported robust earnings recently. We decided to have a deeper look, and we believe that investors might be worried about several concerning factors that we found.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Zooming In On Pinewood Technologies Group's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While it's not a problem to have a positive accrual ratio, indicating a certain level of non-cash profits, a high accrual ratio is arguably a bad thing, because it indicates paper profits are not matched by cash flow. To quote a 2014 paper by Lewellen and Resutek, "firms with higher accruals tend to be less profitable in the future".

Pinewood Technologies Group has an accrual ratio of 0.55 for the year to December 2025. Statistically speaking, that's a real negative for future earnings. To wit, the company did not generate one whit of free cashflow in that time. Even though it reported a profit of UK£50.3m, a look at free cash flow indicates it actually burnt through UK£4.6m in the last year. We saw that FCF was UK£1.7m a year ago though, so Pinewood Technologies Group has at least been able to generate positive FCF in the past. However, that's not the end of the story. We can look at how unusual items in the profit and loss statement impacted its accrual ratio, as well as explore how dilution is impacting shareholders negatively.

Check out our latest analysis for Pinewood Technologies Group

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

In order to understand the potential for per share returns, it is essential to consider how much a company is diluting shareholders. Pinewood Technologies Group expanded the number of shares on issue by 14% over the last year. That means its earnings are split among a greater number of shares. Per share metrics like EPS help us understand how much actual shareholders are benefitting from the company's profits, while the net income level gives us a better view of the company's absolute size. You can see a chart of Pinewood Technologies Group's EPS by clicking here.

A Look At The Impact Of Pinewood Technologies Group's Dilution On Its Earnings Per Share (EPS)

As you can see above, Pinewood Technologies Group has been growing its net income over the last few years, with an annualized gain of 782% over three years. But EPS was only up 487% per year, in the exact same period. And at a glance the 709% gain in profit over the last year impresses. On the other hand, earnings per share are only up 761% in that time. So you can see that the dilution has had a bit of an impact on shareholders.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So it will certainly be a positive for shareholders if Pinewood Technologies Group can grow EPS persistently. However, if its profit increases while its earnings per share stay flat (or even fall) then shareholders might not see much benefit. For that reason, you could say that EPS is more important that net income in the long run, assuming the goal is to assess whether a company's share price might grow.

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

The fact that the company had unusual items boosting profit by UK£54m, in the last year, probably goes some way to explain why its accrual ratio was so weak. We can't deny that higher profits generally leave us optimistic, but we'd prefer it if the profit were to be sustainable. When we crunched the numbers on thousands of publicly listed companies, we found that a boost from unusual items in a given year is often not repeated the next year. And that's as you'd expect, given these boosts are described as 'unusual'. We can see that Pinewood Technologies Group's positive unusual items were quite significant relative to its profit in the year to December 2025. All else being equal, this would likely have the effect of making the statutory profit a poor guide to underlying earnings power.

Our Take On Pinewood Technologies Group's Profit Performance

Pinewood Technologies Group didn't back up its earnings with free cashflow, but this isn't too surprising given profits were inflated by unusual items. The dilution means the results are weaker when viewed from a per-share perspective. On reflection, the above-mentioned factors give us the strong impression that Pinewood Technologies Group'sunderlying earnings power is not as good as it might seem, based on the statutory profit numbers. So while earnings quality is important, it's equally important to consider the risks facing Pinewood Technologies Group at this point in time. You'd be interested to know, that we found 3 warning signs for Pinewood Technologies Group and you'll want to know about these.

Our examination of Pinewood Technologies Group has focussed on certain factors that can make its earnings look better than they are. And, on that basis, we are somewhat skeptical. But there are plenty of other ways to inform your opinion of a company. For example, many people consider a high return on equity as an indication of favorable business economics, while others like to 'follow the money' and search out stocks that insiders are buying. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. We need to see whether the dividend is covered by earnings and if it's growing.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. It paid out 80% of its earnings as dividends last year, which is not unreasonable, but limits reinvestment in the business and leaves the dividend vulnerable to a business downturn. It could become a concern if earnings started to decline. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Danone generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. Dividends consumed 51% of the company's free cash flow last year, which is within a normal range for most dividend-paying organisations.

It's positive to see that Danone's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

See our latest analysis for Danone

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks with flat earnings can still be attractive dividend payers, but it is important to be more conservative with your approach and demand a greater margin for safety when it comes to dividend sustainability. If earnings fall far enough, the company could be forced to cut its dividend. That explains why we're not overly excited about Danone's flat earnings over the past five years. It's better than seeing them drop, certainly, but over the long term, all of the best dividend stocks are able to meaningfully grow their earnings per share.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. In the last 10 years, Danone has lifted its dividend by approximately 4.1% a year on average.

Final Takeaway

Should investors buy Danone for the upcoming dividend? Danone has been unable to generate earnings growth, but at least its dividend looks sustainable, with its profit and cashflow payout ratios within reasonable limits. Bottom line: Danone has some unfortunate characteristics that we think could lead to sub-optimal outcomes for dividend investors.

With that being said, if you're still considering Danone as an investment, you'll find it beneficial to know what risks this stock is facing. Every company has risks, and we've spotted 2 warning signs for Danone you should know about.

data.title: METAWATER Co., Ltd. (TSE:9551) Analysts Are Pretty Bullish On The Stock After Recent Results
data.description:
Last week, you might have seen that METAWATER Co., Ltd. (TSE:9551) released its full-year result to the market. The early response was not positive, with shares down 7.5% to JP¥3,415 in the past week. Results overall were respectable, with statutory earnings of JP¥209 per share roughly in line with what the analysts had forecast. Revenues of JP¥210b came in 3.4% ahead of analyst predictions. This is an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance in its report, look at what experts are forecasting for next year, and see if there has been any change to expectations for the business. So we gathered the latest post-earnings forecasts to see what estimates suggest is in store for next year.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Following the latest results, METAWATER's five analysts are now forecasting revenues of JP¥220.7b in 2027. This would be a modest 5.2% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Per-share earnings are expected to increase 5.0% to JP¥220. Yet prior to the latest earnings, the analysts had been anticipated revenues of JP¥217.4b and earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥209 in 2027. So the consensus seems to have become somewhat more optimistic on METAWATER's earnings potential following these results.

Check out our latest analysis for METAWATER

The consensus price target rose 17% to JP¥3,100, suggesting that higher earnings estimates flow through to the stock's valuation as well. It could also be instructive to look at the range of analyst estimates, to evaluate how different the outlier opinions are from the mean. Currently, the most bullish analyst values METAWATER at JP¥3,800 per share, while the most bearish prices it at JP¥2,000. Analysts definitely have varying views on the business, but the spread of estimates is not wide enough in our view to suggest that extreme outcomes could await METAWATER shareholders.

Looking at the bigger picture now, one of the ways we can make sense of these forecasts is to see how they measure up against both past performance and industry growth estimates. We would highlight that METAWATER's revenue growth is expected to slow, with the forecast 5.2% annualised growth rate until the end of 2027 being well below the historical 9.0% p.a. growth over the last five years. Compare this to the 247 other companies in this industry with analyst coverage, which are forecast to grow their revenue at 5.7% per year. Factoring in the forecast slowdown in growth, it looks like METAWATER is forecast to grow at about the same rate as the wider industry.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest takeaway for us is the consensus earnings per share upgrade, which suggests a clear improvement in sentiment around METAWATER's earnings potential next year. They also reconfirmed their revenue estimates, with the company predicted to grow at about the same rate as the wider industry. We note an upgrade to the price target, suggesting that the analysts believes the intrinsic value of the business is likely to improve over time.

With that said, the long-term trajectory of the company's earnings is a lot more important than next year. At Simply Wall St, we have a full range of analyst estimates for METAWATER going out to 2029, and you can see them free on our platform here..

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider AL Sydbank (CPSE:ALSYDB) After Its 37% One-Year Surge?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether AL Sydbank at around kr.544.50 is still offering value or if most of the opportunity is already priced in, you are in the right place.
  • The share price has moved around recently, with a 3% decline over the last 7 days, an 8.4% gain over the last 30 days, a 4.6% decline year to date, and a 37.2% return over the past year, capped by very large gains over 3 and 5 years.
  • These swings have been accompanied by ongoing interest in the Danish banking sector and investor focus on how balance sheet strength, funding costs, and capital returns shape bank valuations. For AL Sydbank, this context matters because it can influence how the market treats traditional valuation metrics such as P/E, P/B, and dividend yield.
  • AL Sydbank currently has a value score of 3 out of 6. The key question is what different valuation approaches say about that score today, and whether there is an even better way to think about value that will be covered at the end of this article.

Find out why AL Sydbank's 37.2% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: AL Sydbank Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a bank can create over and above the return that shareholders require. It starts with the equity invested in the business and compares the returns generated on that equity to the estimated cost of equity.

For AL Sydbank, the model uses a Book Value of DKK416.84 per share and an Average Return on Equity of 12.81%. This leads to a Stable EPS estimate of DKK59.88 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 4 analysts. The Cost of Equity is set at DKK28.83 per share, which implies an Excess Return of DKK31.05 per share. The Stable Book Value used in the model is DKK467.39 per share, also drawn from weighted future Book Value estimates from 4 analysts.

Combining these inputs, the Excess Returns valuation points to an intrinsic value of roughly DKK1,187.97 per share. With a current share price of around DKK544.50, this corresponds to the stock being 54.2% undervalued according to this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: AL Sydbank Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like AL Sydbank, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is already generating. In general, higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk usually align with a lower, more conservative P/E.

AL Sydbank currently trades on a P/E of 25.37x. This is above the Danish Banks industry average P/E of 11.01x and also above the peer average of 10.10x.
